coredata.dc:title: Role of carbon nanodots in defect passivation and photo-sensitization of mesoscopic-TiO2 for perovskite solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Sidhik, Siraj
Velusamy, Jayaramakrishnan
De la Rosa, Elder
Pérez-García, Sergio Alfonso
Ramos-Ortiz, Gabriel
López-Luke, Tzarara
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a recently developed carbon nanodots (CnDs) were introduced in the mesoscopic TiO2 to form a TiO2: CnDs composite, to be used as an electrode in CH3NH3PbI3 based perovskite devices. It resulted the improvement in morphology of perovskite film and induced a carbon-doping passivating the oxygen defects. In addition, the presence of carbon dots enhanced the charge carrier mobility of TiO2 and provided extra photo-generation ability to the perovskite device due to its absorbing nature. The improved morphology, carbon-doping, photogeneration ability and the improved charge transfer characteristics with the introduction of carbon dots was verified by the scanning electron microscopy (SEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV-visible absorption, X-ray diffraction, space charge limited current (SCLC) measurement, photoluminescence (PL), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), current leakage and open circuit photovoltage decay (OCPD) measurements. An optimized concentration of CnDs (3 wt%) within the TiO2 resulted in a maximum power conversion efficiency of 19.45% with negligible hysteresis, which is 16% higher than the device with pristine TiO2.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Normalized UV-vis absorption (blue line) and PL (red line) spectra of CnDs. The PL spectra with an emission of 476 nm obtained under an excitation of 338 nm. (Inset shows the CnDs water suspension under daylight (left) and hand-held UV lamp (right), (b) High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image (left) of CnDs, and (c) single CnD indicating the inter-planar spacing of the lattice. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) XRD spectra of perovskite films based on pristine TiO2 and TiO2/CnDs ETL; (b) Plot showing the variation of XRD intensity and the FWHM of the primary diffraction peak of perovskite (2θ = 14.2°) as a function of the concentration of CnDs incorporated with the TiO2; (c) UV-visible absorption spectroscopy of pristine TiO2 film and TiO2/CnDs film (The inset shows the Tauc plot of the pristine TiO2 and TiO2/CnDs to extract the bandgap of the material); (d) perovskite films based on pristine TiO2 and TiO2/CnDs ETL. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectra of pristine TiO2, and TiO2: CnDs composite depicting O 1s, Ti 2p and C 1s peaks. Details of the XPS spectra of pristine TiO2 and TiO2: CnDs composite for (b) C 1s, (d) Ti 2p, and (d) O 1s peaks. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Enhancement of charge carrier mobility and reduction of trap states with the introduction of carbon dots, shown by the current-voltage curves of the pristine and carbon-doped mesoporous TiO2 thin films. The inset shows the structure of the device for electron injection. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Color-enhanced cross-sectional SEM image of the complete device architecture, glass/FTO/bl-TiO2/meso-TiO2/perovskite/spiro-MeOTAD/Au, (b) current-voltage characteristic of the perovskite device under AM1.5G irradiation at 100 mWcm−2, (c) IPCE and integrated current density curve of the perovskite solar cell for different concentration of CnDs and, (d) difference in the IPCE curve of perovskite device with pristine TiO2 and TiO2 incorporated with CnDS. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Steady-state photoluminescence spectra of (a) TiO2 samples without and with CnDs(3 wt%), (b) perovskite film based on pristine TiO2 and TiO2: CnDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Nyquist plot of perovskite solar cell based on pristine TiO2 and TiO2: CnDs (3 wt%) (The inset shows the equivalent circuit used for fitting the nyquist plot), (b) Logarithmic J-V characteristics of perovskite devices measured under dark conditions, (c) Open-circuit voltage decay measurement of perovskite device based on pristine TiO2 and TiO2: CnDs (3 wt%), (d) J-V curves of the perovskite device with pristine TiO2 and TiO2: CnDs (3 wt%) under forward and reverse bias. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) The energy-band diagram representing the device architecture of perovskite solar cell with their respective conduction and valence bands, showing the carrier generation and transportation, (b) Schematic representation showing the recombination mechanism due to the presence of deep traps in pristine TiO2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The average photovoltaic parameters of 15 devices with the best cell parameters (in bracket) for different concentration of CnDs in the electron transport layer.

head.ce:title: Role of carbon nanodots in defect passivation and photo-sensitization of mesoscopic-TiO2 for perovskite solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centro de Investigaciones en Optica, A.P. 1-948, Leon, Gto, 37150, Mexico

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centro de Investigación en Materiales Avanzados S. C., Unidad Monterrey-PIIT, Apodaca, Nuevo León, 66628, Mexico

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centro de Investigación en Materiales Avanzados S. C.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Universidad De La Salle Bajio, Campus Campestre, Leon, Gto, 37150, Mexico

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Centro de Investigaciones en Optica, A.P. 1-948, Leon, Gto, 37150, Mexico. .

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a recently developed carbon nanodots (CnDs) were introduced in the mesoscopic TiO2 to form a TiO2: CnDs composite, to be used as an electrode in CH3NH3PbI3 based perovskite devices. It resulted the improvement in morphology of perovskite film and induced a carbon-doping passivating the oxygen defects. In addition, the presence of carbon dots enhanced the charge carrier mobility of TiO2 and provided extra photo-generation ability to the perovskite device due to its absorbing nature. The improved morphology, carbon-doping, photogeneration ability and the improved charge transfer characteristics with the introduction of carbon dots was verified by the scanning electron microscopy (SEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV-visible absorption, X-ray diffraction, space charge limited current (SCLC) measurement, photoluminescence (PL), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), current leakage and open circuit photovoltage decay (OCPD) measurements. An optimized concentration of CnDs (3 wt%) within the TiO2 resulted in a maximum power conversion efficiency of 19.45% with negligible hysteresis, which is 16% higher than the device with pristine TiO2.

ce:section.ce:para: Since 2009, the perovskite solar cells have made a rapid stride with its efficiency rising from 3.8% to 23.3% in few years [1–4]. This rapid surge has attracted the attention of scientific community all over the world resulting in an extraordinary effort of research in the organic-inorganic hybrid perovskite based solar cells [5,6]. The main focus of this research is to attain efficiencies and stability closer or equal to the commercially available crystalline silicon solar cells (26.1%), in order to replace the present silicon devices with a much cheaper perovskite-based solar cells [7–9]. In a race towards attaining higher efficiencies, several architectures of perovskite devices like mesoscopic, inverted-planar, planar, bi-layer and meso-superstructured configurations have been tried and developed [10–14]. Also, several new perovskite materials have been discovered from the basic methylammonium lead iodide to mixed formamidinium based materials. The maximum efficiency of ∼20% for methylammonium lead iodide and 22.7% for the mixed formamidinium based materials have been attained [3,4,15]. The common factor in all the record efficiency perovskite solar cells is the TiO2 as the electron transporting material (ETM). In addition, several other ETMs like ZnO, SnO2, WO3, CdSe and SrTiO3 have been tested to replace the TiO2 achieving comparable efficiencies [16–22].

Despite of all the advantages of TiO2 like appropriate band alignment, low cost, chemical stability, high conductivity and compatibility with various deposition techniques, it exhibits some disadvantages such as point or lattice defects like oxygen vacancies, Ti3+ states etc., which act as recombination centers for the injected electrons [23–26]. These defect states result in reduced efficiency [27–29]. Hence the fabrication of TiO2, free of defects is critical for improving the charge-carrier extraction and transportation, and in turn to enhance the device efficiency.

There have been several reports of improving the electronic properties of TiO2 by doping with suitable metal and non-metal ions. In 2016, Gratzel's group reported the use of Li+ ion as a dopant in TiO2 to passivate the electronic trap states enabling faster electron transport in perovskite solar cells [30]. Recently, Yin et al., reported the use of Nb-doped TiO2 showing higher conductivity, higher mobility, and lower trap-state density [31,32] for efficient electron injection and transportation attaining an efficiency of 19% in perovskite devices [33]. Very recently, our group has shown that the incorporation of Co(III) ions in the TiO2 lattice would passivate the oxygen vacancies and increase the conductivity leading to improved electron transport [28]. In addition, the use of Al, Zn, Y, Fe, F, N and Mg-doped TiO2 have also been reported for perovskite-based solar cells [34–38]. However, there have been only few reports of carbon-doped TiO2 being utilized in solar cells [39–41]. As per the literature, the carbon dopant acting as an anion replaces the oxygen in the TiO2 lattice and as a cation occupies the interstitial lattice sites [42,43]. The TiCTi structure was formed in conditions like flame pyrolysis of Ti metal sheet, annealing of TiC powders, and ion-assisted electron beam evaporation [40]. While the Ti O C structure was obtained during the sol-gel process with carbon precursors or high-temperature reactions of TiO2 with carbon precursors [41]. Carbon nanodots (CnDs) are an exciting new class of carbon nanomaterials, due to its unique optical, electronic, and physicochemical properties finding numerous applications in optoelectronics, photocatalysis, photovoltaics, bioimaging and biosensing [44]. Recently, these CnDs have been employed in perovskite solar cells as a hole transporting material, passivation of the surface defects in perovskite film and as a dopant for planar perovskite devices [45–47]. However, the influence of these CnDs on the optoelectronic properties of mesoscopic perovskite device by forming a mesoporous TiO2/CnDs composite needs to be elucidated.

In this work, the CnDs were synthesized and introduced within the TiO2 solution to obtain a thin mesoporous film, followed by the sintering process (450 °C) to convert the TiO2 into anatase phase [48]. Recent reports from Wang et al., and Asadzadeh et al., utilizes the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) to report that a temperature above 550 °C was required to decompose the fluorescent carbon dots [49,50]. Hence, the CnDs within the mesoporous TiO2 film would remain intact even after the sintering process of 450 °C. They also observed that the oxygen containing groups surrounding the CnDs would decompose at 200–500 °C which would result in the release of sp3 hybridized carbon into the TiO2 lattice [49,50]. The presence of CnDs within the TiO2 was verified by the variation in roughness, absorption, bandgap, XPS and ΔIPCE measurements. The introduction of carbon dots resulted in an improvement of morphology of the perovskite film by increasing the grain size and reducing the root mean square roughness. In addition, it resulted in carbon doping from the sp3-hybridized carbon groups surrounding the carbon dot, forming a TiOC structure resulting in the passivation of oxygen defects as verified by the XPS and SCLC measurements, while the sp2 hybridized carbon nanodot core remains intact within the TiO2 as verified by Wang et al. [49]. An increment in the electron mobility was observed with the introduction of carbon dots as verified by the SCLC measurement. In addition to the passivation effect and increment in carrier mobility, the contribution of the absorbing nature of CnDs on the photocurrent was confirmed by the difference in the incident photon conversion efficiency measurement (ΔIPCE) [47]. The photogeneration ability of CnDs, increment in the conductivity of TiO2: CnDs composite and the defect passivation of TiO2 (achieved by carbon doping), resulted in an enhancement of current density (Jsc), open-circuit voltage (Voc) and fill factor (FF) followed by an increase in PCE. An optimized concentration of CnDs (3 wt%) provided a maximum PCE of 19.45% corresponding to 11% increase in the Jsc, 70 mV increase in the Voc and 10% increment in the FF when compared to the perovskite devices with pristine TiO2.

The carbon nanodots (CnDs) were synthesized using a one-pot thermolysis method using citric acid (CA) and 2-Imidazolidinethione (2-IZT) as the precursors, which have been recently reported by our group [48]. The detailed synthesis process is presented in the experimental section of the supplementary information. Fig. 1 (a) represents the UV-visible absorption spectrum of CnDs suspended in water (blue curve). The absorption peak observed at 245 nm (5.06 eV) corresponds to an electronic transition (S0 → S2) for the prepared CnDs. This transition is typically ascribed as π-π* transition within the sp2- localized carbon core of CnDs. The red curve in Fig. 1 (a) depicts the photoluminescence (PL) spectra from the CnDs, with an emission peak at 476 nm obtained under an excitation of 338 nm. The size distribution and morphology of the CnDs were studied using a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) as shown in Fig. 1(b and c). These CnDs were observed to have an average diameter of ∼3.5 ± 0.4 nm. The interplanar spacing of the CnDs observed from the HRTEM images was found to be 0.233 nm which is consistent with the (002) plane of the amorphous carbon comprising aromatic carbon arranged in significantly random manner. The synthesized CnDs comprises of sp3 hybridized carbon with hanging bonds and uncoordinated carbon atoms, surrounding the sp2 bonded carbon core present in a hexagonal lattice [48].

Further, these CnDs were introduced in a TiO2 solution at different concentration (0, 1, 3, and 5 wt%), followed by the spin-coating process to produce a thin film of TiO2: CnDs composite. Finally, the CnD incorporated TiO2 thin films were sintered at 450 °C for 30 min. The detailed experimental procedure is presented in the experimental section of the supplementary information. The perovskite/TiO2 interface plays a vital role in high-efficiency perovskite solar cells, where a smooth, uniform and pin-hole free surface is required for effective charge-carrier extraction and transport. In order to study the morphology, AFM images of TiO2 film with different concentration of CnDs and the perovskite film deposited on top of mesoporous TiO2 incorporated with different concentration of CnDs were recorded and the root mean square (RMS) roughness was calculated (see Figs. S1 and S2). The RMS roughness of the TiO2 films with different concentration of CnDs was calculated to be 20.379 ± 0.3 nm (0 wt%), 21.935 ± 0.5 nm (1 wt%), 23.477 ± 0.3 nm (3 wt%), and 25.224 ± 0.6 nm (5 wt%). The slight increment in the RMS value of the mesoporous TiO2 film with the increasing concentration of CnDs, can be attributed to the presence of smaller carbon dots (∼3.54 nm) within the TiO2. The point to be noted is that, the film remains smooth and uniform even after the introduction of CnDs. Further, the RMS roughness of the perovskite film deposited on top of mesoporous TiO2 with different concentration of CnDs was calculated to be 17.106 ± 0.6 nm (0 wt%), 15.326 ± 0.7 nm (1 wt%), 14.687 ± 0.3 nm (3 wt%), and 14.433 + 0.5 nm (5 wt%). The grain size of the perovskite film was also observed to increase with the use of different electron transport layer (ETL). This difference in morphology of the perovskite film indicates that the underlying ETL surface influences the nucleation and growth of the perovskite grains. This could be attributed to significant changes in the surface energy of ETL achieved with the introduction of CnDs leading to a perovskite crystal growth preferably due to the Frank–van der Merwe mode [51]. In this mode, the adatoms attached to the surface sites result in atomically smooth and fully formed layers similar to what is observed in the perovskite film fabricated on the modified ETL surface (see Fig. S2). The improved morphology and the increase in grain size of the perovskite film from ∼150 nm (pristine) to ∼400 nm (3 wt%) was also verified by the SEM image (see Figs. S3c–d). Fig. S4 depicts the cross-sectional SEM images of the perovskite film deposited on mesoporous TiO2 with different concentration of CnDs. The thickness of perovskite film was found to be similar in all the cases (∼250 nm), thus neglecting its effect on any variation in absorption. Also, the distribution and variation in grain size for all the samples are in relation with the surficial AFM and SEM image of the samples with and without CnDs. This improvement in morphology of perovskite layer with the reduction in roughness proves to be advantageous for the solar cell performance by improving its charge transport characteristics.

The optoelectronic properties of TiO2 film and the TiO2/perovskite interface were studied. The X-Ray diffraction (XRD) and UV-visible absorption spectroscopic measurements were carried out to explore the crystalline and absorption properties. Fig. S5 represents the XRD spectra of the pristine TiO2, and TiO2: CnDs composite. The pristine TiO2 shows identical crystalline characteristics with the TiO2: CnDs composite. Both the films maintain the anatase phase of TiO2 with similar crystallinity and crystal size. Further, the XRD spectra of the perovskite film based on pristine TiO2 and TiO2: CnDs composite were recorded and shown in Fig. 2 (a). The formation of CH3NH3PbI3 based perovskite was verified by the diffraction peaks at 14.2°, 23.5°, 28.4°, 31.8°, 34.8°, 40.5° and 43.2° (see Fig. 2(a)). In addition, a small diffraction peak observed at 12.8° indicates a very small amount of PbI2 in the perovskite film. The low intensity of this peak is an indication of the complete conversion of the perovskite which is suitable for high-efficiency perovskite devices. The perovskite film deposited on top of TiO2: CnDs shows stronger diffraction peaks at 14.2°, 23.5°, 31.8°, and 34.8° indicating enhanced crystallinity. Further, the variation in the diffraction peak intensity and full width half maximum (FWHM) of the primary peak (2θ = 14.2°) as a function of concentration of CnDs is depicted in Fig. 2(b). The diffraction peak intensity was observed to increase with the increase in concentration of CnDs; with the maximum intensity being obtained for 3.0 wt% of CnDs showing enhanced crystalline nature of the obtained perovskite layer. The FWHM of the primary peak was observed to decrease with the rise in the concentration of CnDs. The reduced value of the FWHM denotes an increased crystal size within the grains of perovskite film. This increase in the crystal size can be related to the enlargement in grain size of the perovskite film verified by the AFM and the SEM images.

Fig. 2(c) depicts the UV-visible absorption of pristine and TiO2: CnDs composite. The TiO2: CnDs sample shows enhanced absorption in the UV region (300–440 nm) compared to the pristine TiO2 preferably due to the absorbing nature of carbon dots within the TiO2, since no variation in the crystallinity of TiO2 was observed with the introduction of CnDs (see Fig. S5). In addition, the bandgap of the corresponding films were extracted from the UV-visible absorption spectrum and is depicted in the inset of Fig. 2(c). The introduction of carbon dots resulted in a decrease of bandgap from 3.56 to 3.51 eV. The deeper conduction band minimum of the TiO2 will increase the driving force of the charge transport from the perovskite to the TiO2. Fig. 2(d) depicts the UV-visible absorption spectra of the perovskite film with and without the CnDs. The perovskite film based on TiO2: CnDs shows a slightly higher absorption for wavelength less than 700 nm when compared to the perovskite film on pristine TiO2. This increase in absorption can be related to the improved morphology, crystallinity of the perovskite film and also the absorption of carbon dots present within the TiO2 layer.

X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out to confirm the presence and state of the elements in the pristine TiO2 and TiO2: CnDs composite. Fig. 3 (a) depicts the XPS survey of pristine TiO2 and TiO2: CnDs composite showing the respective elemental peaks, O 1s, Ti 2p and C 1s. The C 1s showed an enhanced peak for the TiO2: CnDs composite indicating the presence of more amount of carbon within the film, which could be related to the presence of CnDs. In addition, a high-resolution spectrum of the C 1s was obtained and depicted in Fig. 3(b). The C 1s spectra provides three different peaks centered at 288.24 eV, 285.50 eV, and 284.50 eV corresponding to C=O, CO and CC, respectively [47]. The TiO2: CnDs composite shows a stronger C 1s peaks compared to the pristine TiO2, which could be suggesting the presence of CnDs within the TiO2 film. The weaker C 1s peak obtained for the pristine TiO2 corresponds to the carbon contamination which can be observed in all material's surface. And, since both samples are fabricated and measured under similar conditions, the relative increase in the C 1s peak for the TiO2: CnDs composite could be attributed to the presence of carbon dots within the sample.

Fig. 3(c) depicts the high-resolution spectra of Ti 2p for the pristine TiO2 and TiO2: CnDs composite. In the spectrum, the doublets at 463.9 eV and 458.1eV corresponding to Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2 are related with the spin-orbital splitting [52]. These peaks represent the Ti4+ states in the TiO2 lattice [52]. After the introduction of carbon dots in the TiO2 film, a small shift in the position of Ti 2p peaks along with a reduction in the area has been observed. The peaks corresponding to TiO2 incorporating carbon dots are now located at 463.7 eV and 457.8 eV. This shift in the position of peaks can be attributed to the substitution of Ti by the C ions within the lattice presenting a doping effect [28,52]. Also, the introduction of carbon dots results in a decrease in the area of Ti4+ peaks, indicating a reduction of TiO2 in the film, due to the formation of TiOC structure within the lattice with the substitution of the Ti ions with the carbon [42,52].

Next, the high-resolution O 1s spectrum of pristine TiO2 and TiO2: CnDs composite is presented in Fig. 3(d). The two peaks observed at 530.8 eV and 529.3eV corresponds to the non-lattice oxygen and the lattice oxygen [52]. The carbon dot incorporated TiO2 shows a less pronounced shoulder at 530.8 eV and an increased area at 529.3 eV compared to the pristine TiO2. The decrease in the shoulder of non-lattice oxygen can be attributed to the reduction in the oxygen vacancies on the surface [52]. Also, the increase in the area of lattice oxygen is a proof for the reduction of oxygen vacancies within the lattice by the replacement of the interstitial defects like Ti (III) ions by the C (IV) ions attracting more oxygen into the lattice [43]. Hence, we can presume that the incorporation of carbon dots in the TiO2 film improves the electronic properties of the film by passivating the oxygen vacancies by carbon doping. This carbon doping could have been achieved by the high density of sp [3] hybridized uncoordinated carbon atoms present on the sp [2] hybridized carbon core [48].

The electron mobility of the respective films was investigated using space charge limited current (SCLC) measurements [28,34]. This method is helpful in elucidating and comparing the bulk charge transport behavior of the TiO2 layers [34]. For the SCLC measurement, an electron-only diode device which comprises mesoporous TiO2 film with and without the CnDs deposited on FTO substrates was utilized, to enable the electron injection (see inset, Fig. 3). The respective films were prepared by spin-coating technique followed by annealing at 450 °C for 30 min. Finally, Au was evaporated as the counter electrode on the prepared TiO2 films. Fig. 4 represent the current-voltage (J-V) curve of the fabricated TiO2 thin films with different concentration of CnDs. It consists of two regimes, ohmic regime at lower voltages and SCLC regime at higher voltages [28]. The ohmic regime shows a linear dependence of current with the voltage which can be utilized to elucidate the charge carrier mobility [28]. The Mott-Gurney law was utilized to find the electron mobility of the respective films,
J =
9
εε
r
μ
e
V
2
8 L
3
, where ɛ is the dielectric constant which is 55 for TiO2, ɛ
r is the free space permittivity, μ
e is the electron mobility, and L is the thickness of the TiO2 film (∼150 nm). As indicated in Fig. 4, the introduction of CnDs resulted in a higher current with the extracted electron mobility of 2.23 × 10−5 cm2 V−1 s−1 for 1 wt%, 7.15 × 10−5 cm2 V−1 s−1 for 3 wt%, and 5.11 × 10−5 cm2 V−1 s−1 for 5 wt% higher than the pristine TiO2 which is having an electron mobility of 8.52 × 10−6 cm2 V−1 s−1.

Moving towards the SCLC regime at higher voltages, the pristine TiO2 (0 wt%) shows a quadratic dependence of current with the voltage indicating the presence of space charges, in this case oxygen vacancies as verified by the XPS measurements [28,34]. With the addition of CnDs (1 wt% and 3 wt%), the J-V curve in the SCLC regime starts becoming linear, depicting velocity-saturated conduction due to the suppression of space charge regions [34,37]. With further increase in the concentration of carbon dots (5 wt%), the ohmic nature of the J-V curve is maintained but the mobility happens to decrease as calculated above. It indicates the suppression of defects, but the excess of carbon dots act as scattering point for charges leading to reduced charge-carrier mobility. This SCLC measurement can be further utilized to find the trap density using the ohmic to trap filled limit transition point (VTFL), which is related by
V
TFL
=
eN
t
L
2
2
εε
0
, where e is the charge of an electron, and Nt is the trap density. The calculated trap density for CnD incorporated TiO2 was 5.75 × 1017 cm−3 for 1 wt%, 2.2 × 1017 cm−3 for 3 wt% and 1.45 × 1017 cm−3 for 5 wt% which happens to be lower than the pristine TiO2 with the trap density of 8.4 × 1017 cm−3. All these indicate that the addition of carbon dots results in the enhancement of charge carrier mobility with the suppression of trap states.

Perovskite devices with the architecture FTO/bl-TiO2/meso-TiO2/CH3NH3PbI3/spiro-MeOTAD/Au were fabricated to study the influence of CnDs on the performance of the solar cell. Fig. 5 (a) represents the color enhanced cross-sectional scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of the complete device architecture. The optimized perovskite device comprises 50 nm thick compact TiO2 layer, 150 nm thick mesoporous TiO2 layer, 250 nm thick perovskite capping layer, 200 nm thick spiro-MeOTAD layer and 60 nm Au electrode. Different concentration of CnDs (0 wt%, 1 wt%, 3 wt% and 5 wt%) were utilized to optimize the photovoltaic performance of the device. Fig. 5(b) represents the J-V curve of perovskite devices with different concentration of CnDs, obtained by reverse bias scanning at a scan rate of 100 mV/s under simulated irradiation of AM 1.5G (100 mW/cm2). The average photovoltaic parameters and the statistical data of 15 devices and the best cell parameter extracted from the J-V measurements were recorded in the Table 1 and Fig. S6, respectively.

The perovskite device with the pristine TiO2 provides average photo conversion efficiency (PCE) of 15.60% with a current density (Jsc) of 20.91 mA/cm2, open-circuit voltage (Voc) of 1.037 V, and fill factor (FF) of 68.69. When compared to the reference device (0 wt%), the increment in efficiency for devices with CnDs was mainly due to an increase in Jsc and the F.F. The highest average power conversion efficiency of 18.82% was obtained with the incorporation of 3 wt% of CnDs, corresponding to an average Jsc of 23.22 mA/cm2, Voc of 1.081 V and F·F of 74.71. If the concentration of CnDs is increased beyond 3 wt%, the photovoltaic performance starts degrading. A concentration of 5 wt% yields an average PCE of 17.05%, with a corresponding average Jsc of 21.96 mA/cm2, Voc of 1.062 V and F·F of 72.99. The degradation in performance of the perovskite device with 5.0 wt% of CnDs can be related to the reduced mobility of TiO2 as verified by the SCLC measurements. Even though, the introduction of 5 wt% of CnDs results in much reduced trap state density, the electron mobility becomes lower than the perovskite device with 3.0 wt% of carbon dots. This reduction in mobility would inflict the charge transfer from the photo-active layer to the TiO2 resulting in a decreased power conversion efficiency. The maximum PCE obtained for the best cell, for each of the doping concentration follows the similar trend as the average efficiency. It can also be observed that the perovskite devices based on CnDs show higher reproducibility compared to the devices with pristine TiO2 (see Fig. S6).

In order to confirm the increment in Jsc, the incident photon conversion efficiency (IPCE) curves were measured for the optimized device and depicted in Fig. 5(c). The IPCE curve presents a wide band photo-response from 350 to 800 nm, which completely matches with the absorption spectrum of CH3NH3PbI3 material. The variation in the IPCE is completely consistent with the variation of photocurrent observed in the J-V curves, with the maximum IPCE response being obtained for perovskite devices with 3 wt% of CnDs. The integrated Jsc was estimated from the IPCE curves by integrating the product of solar spectral curve with the measured IPCE obtained under low illumination (see Fig. 5(c)). It is in good agreement with the measured photocurrent derived from the J-V curves. Fig. 5(d) represents the difference of IPCE curve (ΔIPCE) for perovskite devices (1 wt%, 3 wt% and 5 wt%) with respect to the reference device (0 wt%). The perovskite device shows two distinct patterns of ΔIPCE; a small band of around ∼60% from 300 to 400 nm centered at ∼330 nm and the second band of less than 10% from 400 to 800 nm maintaining a constant height throughout the band. The first band of ΔIPCE observed from 300 to 400 nm exactly matches with the absorption band of CnDs centered at 338 nm (see Fig. 1(a)) indicating the photogeneration ability of the respective carbon dots within the device. The other part of ΔIPCE could be due to the improved extraction of electron achieved by the improvement in morphology, passivation of defects and improved charge-carrier mobility due to carbon doping. This can be confirmed by considering the intensity of ΔIPCE band at ∼300–400 nm. The device with 3 wt% of CnDs shows a higher intensity of ΔIPCE, while the device with 5 wt%, even though having a higher concentration of CnDs have lesser intensity than the 3 wt%. It is due to the reduced charge transport characteristics of the respective film as observed in the SCLC measurement (see Fig. 4). The device with 1 wt% carbon dots shows much lower intensity of ΔIPCE due to the lower concentration of carbon dots and also the lower electron mobility of the film. Hence we can presume that, the improvement in performance of the device due to the increase in Jsc and FF can be attributed to improved morphology, photogeneration ability of the carbon dots and the improved charge transfer characteristics achieved by defect passivation and improved charge carrier mobility. The distinct hump like pattern observed in the ΔIPCE curve (300–400 nm) is a clear indication that not all the CnDs within the TiO2 could have been burnt and enough of them are present to give additional photogenerative ability to the perovskite solar cells as explained earlier.

A better understanding of the charge carrier transfer and recombination is very much important, as it influences the photo-electrochemical properties and the performance of photovoltaic devices. The photoluminescence (PL) is an efficient tool to investigate the efficiency of charge migration, trapping, and transfer, while understanding the fate of the generated electrons and holes in a semiconductor material [13,28]. A PL emission occurs due to the recombination of excited electrons and holes. Fig. 6 (a) depicts the steady-state PL spectra of pristine TiO2 and TiO2: CnDs composite, respectively. This PL at ∼530 nm corresponds to the recombination of carriers from some intermediate levels or trap states lying within the energy bands of the TiO2 (arising due to some oxygen vacancies or interstitial defects). The pristine meso-TiO2 shows higher PL intensity, indicating the presence of defect states, also verified by the XPS and SCLC measurements. The PL intensity was observed to decrease with the introduction of CnDs within the TiO2. It can be attributed to the suppression of these intermediate defect states achieved by carbon doping within the TiO2 lattice, as verified by the XPS and SCLC measurement [13,28].

When in contact with the charge transport layers, the perovskite film shows strong PL quenching due to an efficient charge transfer from the photoactive layer to the transport layers. Fig. 6(b) represents the PL spectra of perovskite film fabricated on top of pristine TiO2 and TiO2: CnDs composite. A clear quenching of the PL emission was observed for the TiO2: CnDs(3.0 wt%) composite when compared to the meso-TiO2. This can be attributed to a more effective charge-carrier extraction of mesoporous TiO2 with the introduction of CnDs. Hence, it can be inferred that the carbon dot-incorporated TiO2 film improves the charge extraction from the active material and suppresses the carrier recombination at the interface due to the passivation of oxygen defects, leading to enhanced Jsc and FF [13,28]. Further increase in the concentration of CnDs (5.0 wt%), results in a slightly enhanced PL emission indicating reduced charge extraction ability of the TiO2 with 5.0 wt% CnDs and enhanced recombination. The excess of carbon within the TiO2 would serve as a recombination center where the carriers would be trapped leading to its recombination, and the degradation of the photovoltaic performance. Hence, it can be inferred that the CnDs-incorporated TiO2 film (until 3.0 wt%) not only improves the charge extraction from the active material and suppresses the carrier recombination at the interface due to the passivation of oxygen defects but also enhances the mobility, leading to enhanced improved Jsc and FF in the device.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out to elucidate the series resistance (Rs) and the charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the perovskite solar cells [28]. Fig. 7 (a) depicts the Nyquist plot for perovskite devices with pristine TiO2 and TiO2: CnDs (3 wt%) under one sun illumination measured at 0.8 V bias. The equivalent circuit shown in the inset of Fig. 7(a) shows the series resistance Rs which depends on the FTO substrate and the external wire contact, Rct represents the charge transfer resistance of the FTO/TiO2/perovskite/HTM interfaces, and C is the capacitance. In this case, only the interface between TiO2 and perovskite is modified, and hence Rct will depend on the variation of resistance at this interface. Therefore, the only semi-circle resolved from EIS spectra (shown in Fig. 7(a)) corresponds to the charge transfer behavior between the perovskite and TiO2 interface. According to the literature, the contact resistance (Rs) can be extracted from the intercept in the x-axis of the high-frequency curve. The values of Rs for both these devices were almost similar, indicating the negligible effect of carbon doping on the contact resistance. The value of the charge transfer resistance (Rct) of CnDs incorporated TiO2 (40.8 Ω) is lower than the device with the pristine TiO2 (53.2 Ω). In addition, the variation of Rct as a function of bias voltages is presented in Fig. S7. From the obtained EIS results, it can be presumed that the CnD incorporation of mesoporous TiO2 lowers the interfacial charge-transfer resistance, hence providing an accelerated charge transfer from the perovskite to the TiO2 due to the increased charge-carrier mobility of the TiO2 [28,52]. Additionally, a reduction in leakage current for the perovskite device with carbon dots was confirmed by the current-voltage measurements in the dark, as shown in Fig. 7(b). The current density value measured under reverse bias, decreases by almost two orders of magnitude for perovskite devices with carbon dots when compared to the device with pristine TiO2 54.

Further, the open-circuit photovoltage decay (OCPD) measurements were carried out to elucidate the recombination kinetics of the respective perovskite devices. These measurements were carried out by obtaining the steady state V oc under solar light illumination followed by monitoring the decay of the V oc, when the light is turned off. It is a known fact that the presence of defect states or space charge region increases the chances of recombination leading to a faster decay of the V oc 53. As shown in Fig. 7(c), the V oc of the perovskite device with CnDs (3 wt%) had a slower photovoltage decay than the reference (pristine), signifying a lower rate of charge recombination due to passivation of interstitial defects within the TiO2 by carbon doping [53].

Fig. 7(d) represents the current-voltage curve of one of the cells with pristine TiO2 and TiO2:CnDs (3 wt%) measured in forward and reverse scan directions with the key photovoltaic parameters. The perovskite device with the pristine TiO2 shows clear hysteresis, whereas the carbon dot incorporated device shows almost negligible hysteresis. The device based on pristine TiO2 gives a PCE of 16.24% for a corresponding Jsc of 22.01 mA/cm2, Voc of 1.057 V, and FF of 69.8% in the forward scan direction. For a reverse scan direction, the PCE changes to 14.76% with a Jsc of 21.72 mA/cm2, Voc of 1.033 V and FF of 65.7%. When the carbon dot incorporated TiO2(3 wt%) was used, the J-V curves almost overlapped each other indicating reduced hysteresis with a PCE of 18.87% for forward scan and 18.62% for the reverse scan. A significant reduction in the hysteresis phenomenon can be attributed to the reduced trap density or space charge within the carbon dot incorporated TiO2. From all the above characterization, it can be perceived that the Jsc increases due to the photogeneration ability of CnDs and the improved conductivity of carbon-doped TiO2, and the FF with the improved charge transfer and injection characteristics due to passivation of oxygen vacancies, leading to enhanced PCE. The stability of perovskite device with and without the CnDs was monitored for a period of 10 days and the variation in power conversion efficiency was plotted in Fig. S8. The devices were exposed to ambient environment without encapsulation for a relative humidity of 40–50% at 30 °C. It has been demonstrated that the degradation of perovskite solar cells in air is mainly influenced by the decomposition of the perovskite material [54]. As indicated in Fig. S8 both the devices with and without the carbon dots exhibited similar performance degradation retaining ∼40% of the overall efficiency after a span of 10 days.

Based on the above characterization and findings, a band-diagram representation of the perovskite device architecture with CnDs and a schematic representation of the possible recombination phenomenon avoided by the introduction of carbon dots (in red) have been represented in Fig. 8 . The energy level of the corresponding CnDs was estimated by the linear fitting of Mott-schottky curve obtained from the electrochemical impedance measurement [55] (see Fig. S9). The details of the calculation are provided in the Supplementary information. The position of the conduction band (4.0 eV) was evaluated from the curve. The bandgap was obtained from the derivative of absorption spectra which was found to be ∼4 eV. Finally, a band diagram representation of the entire device architecture with their corresponding valence and conduction band levels are represented in Fig. 8(a) [56–58]. As observed, the CnDs and the perovskite absorbs the sunlight resulting in the generation of charge carriers, which are separated along the device with the electron being transported through the TiO2 to the external circuit and the holes being transported through the HTM to the counter electrode. Hence, the total photocurrent generated within the system is the sum of photocurrent from the CnDs due to its absorption in the wavelength range of 300–400 nm and photocurrent from the perovskite due to its broad band absorption from 300 to 850 nm. Two different pathways for the electrons generated in the perovskite can also be proposed, one is the direct transfer towards the TiO2 and the second is the transit through the CnDs, leading to the TiO2. In addition, a schematic diagram depicting the passivation effect of carbon doping in TiO2 is presented in Fig. 7(b). The point defects in this case, the oxygen vacancies within the pristine TiO2, results in deep energy levels below the CB of the TiO2. These energy levels act as a cascading path for the recombination of injected electrons with the holes present in the VB of the perovskite absorber and spiro-MeOTAD. The introduction of carbon in the TiO2 lattice passivates the oxygen defects, thus avoiding the recombination process leading to an enhanced PV performance with negligible hysteresis.

In summary, methylammonium lead iodide based perovskite solar cells (FTO/TiO2:CnDs/CH3NH3PbI3/spiro-MeOTAD/Au) with different concentration of CnDs in TiO2 were fabricated. The introduction of CnDs resulted for an improvement in morphology of perovskite film by increasing the grain size and reducing the RMS value. In addition, carbon doping within the TiO2 was achieved by forming a Ti O C structure. The carbon doping results in the passivation of oxygen defects as verified by the XPS and SCLC measurements. The presence of carbon dots also increases the charge carrier mobility of TiO2 as verified by the SCLC measurements. In addition to the passivation effect, the photogeneration ability of CnDs due to its absorption in the UV region has also been observed. The CnD based perovskite device shows reduced recombination and improved charge-carrier transfer characteristics, confirmed by the PL, OCPD, EIS and dark current-voltage measurements. The optimized CnD based perovskite device shows an improvement of 16% yielding a maximum efficiency of 19.45% with negligible hysteresis. This increment in efficiency was mainly contributed by an increase in the photocurrent of ∼11%, open-circuit voltage of 70 mV and the fill factor of ∼10%, achieved by the improved morphology, extra photogeneration ability, enhanced charge carrier mobility and the defect passivation by the carbon doping.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge financial support from CONACYT through grant 259192, the CEMIE-Solar (207450) consortium projects P27 and P28 and LANIAUTO through grant 294030. One of the authors (Siraj Sidhik) acknowledges the Doctoral fellowship from SENER-CONACYT. We also thank Christian Albor and Carlos Juarez for technical support. We would also like to thank the technical staff of CIMAV Campus Monterrey, Luis Gerardo Silva Vidaurri for XPS measurements.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

Experimental details and the characterization techniques used, AFM images of mesoporous TiO2 films with different concentration of CnDs, AFM images of perovskite film deposited on top of mesoporous TiO2 incorporated with different concentration of CnDs, Surficial images of pristine TiO2 film, TiO2: CnDs film, and perovskite films based on pristine TiO2, and TiO2: CnDs electron transporting material, Cross-sectional SEM images of perovskite film fabricated by one step spin coating method with different concentration of CnDs, XRD and UV-Visible spectra of pristine TiO2 film, TiO2: CnDs film and perovskite films based on pristine TiO2, and TiO2: CnDs electron transporting material, Device statistics of perovskite solar cell employing ETL incorporated with different concentration of carbon nanodots, Plot showing the variation of charge transfer resistance (Rct) extracted from the Nyquist plot, Plot showing the variation of normalized power conversion efficiency as a function of time.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2019.01.105.
